sentences.base_language: Marco's phone rings on Saturday morning.
sentences.target_language: El teléfono de Marco suena el sábado por la mañana.
sentences.grammarNote:
Possession in Spanish uses **de**: **el teléfono de Marco** = Marco's phone (literally 'the phone of Marco'). There is no apostrophe-s in Spanish.
**el sábado** = on Saturday — days of the week use the definite article **el/la**: **el lunes** (on Monday) · **los sábados** (on Saturdays).

Phone greetings vary by region:
**¿Aló?** — common in Latin America
**¿Diga? / ¿Dígame?** — Spain (literally 'speak to me')
**¿Bueno?** — Mexico

sentences.base_language: 'Hi Marco, it's Sofía! How are you?'
sentences.grammarNote:
**soy Sofía** — identifying yourself by name uses **ser**: **soy yo** (it's me) · **soy Sofía** (it's Sofía).
**¿Cómo estás?** — informal, using **tú** form. Between friends this is natural; with a stranger you'd say **¿Cómo está usted?**

**Muy bien** — the subject **estoy** is dropped entirely: understood from context.
**¿Y tú?** = And you? — subject pronoun **tú** is kept here because there's no verb — it IS the whole question.

sentences.base_language: 'Good! I was calling to see if you have plans this weekend.'
sentences.target_language: —¡Bien! Te llamo para ver si tienes planes este fin de semana.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Te llamo para ver si...** — **llamo** is present tense used to explain the reason for a call (like English 'I'm calling to...'). **Para + infinitive** expresses purpose: **para ver** (to see), **para preguntar** (to ask), **para invitarte** (to invite you).
**si** (no accent) = if/whether in indirect questions — different from **sí** (yes).

sentences.base_language: 'Not really. What did you have in mind?'
sentences.grammarNote:
**La verdad que no** = not really — literally 'the truth is no'. A very natural hedge.
**¿Qué tienes pensado?** = What did you have in mind? — literally 'What do you have thought/planned?'. **Pensado** is a past participle used as an adjective after **tener**.

sentences.base_language: 'How about we meet up for coffee tomorrow? There's a new café near the plaza.'
sentences.target_language: —¿Qué tal si quedamos para tomar un café mañana? Hay un café nuevo cerca de la plaza.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Quedar** = to meet up — the go-to verb for making plans in Spanish. **¿Quedamos?** = Shall we meet up? Much more natural than **encontrarse** in casual conversation.
**¿Qué tal si...?** = How about if...? / What if...? — a common way to make suggestions.
**Hay** = there is — impersonal form of **haber**, invariable for singular and plural.

sentences.base_language: 'Sounds great! What time works for you?'
sentences.target_language: —¡Me parece bien! ¿A qué hora te viene bien?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me parece bien** = it seems good to me = sounds good — **parecer** works like **gustar**: **me parece** (it seems to me).
**¿A qué hora te viene bien?** = What time works for you? — **venir bien** = to suit / to work for someone (literally 'to come well').

sentences.base_language: 'Around eleven? Or is that too early for you?'
sentences.target_language: —¿Sobre las once? ¿O es muy temprano para ti?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sobre** + time = around / approximately: **sobre las once** = around eleven.
Alternatives: **a eso de las once** or **más o menos a las once** — all common in casual speech.
**para ti** = for you — **ti** is the stressed form of **tú** used after prepositions (except **con**: **contigo**).

**Las once está perfecto** — note **está** (singular) even though **las once** looks plural. Time expressions are grammatically treated as a singular block: **las once está bien**.
**Hasta mañana** = see you tomorrow — literally 'until tomorrow'. One of the most common farewells.

sentences.base_language: 'Perfect! I'll send you the address. Bye!'
sentences.grammarNote:
**Te mando** = I'll send you — present tense used for an immediate future intention, very natural in Spanish.
**te** = indirect object pronoun (to you): **Te mando** = I send to you.
**¡Chao!** = bye! — from Italian *ciao*, widely used across Latin America. Also spelled **chau**.
